text:
Problem Overview

The introduction for Investigation 2 introduces the key term, theoretical probability. Theoretical probabilities are similar to experimental probabilities. But to determine the number of outcomes for experimental probabilities, like heads occurring in a coin toss, you conduct an experiment. The outcomes for theoretical probabilities are determined by examining the possible outcomes. Then you analyze and count the number of favorable and non-favorable events. For example, if a coin is tossed, there are two outcomes – a head or tail occurs. Each has the same chance of occurring on any toss of the coin. As we conduct more and more trials, the experimental probability will get closer to the theoretical probability, or ½ in the case of tossing a fair coin.

In Problem 2.1, students choose colored chips from a container to determine experimental probabilities. They find the theoretical probabilities by counting the number of each color and then finding the fraction of chips of a certain color in the container. Then, students contrast the theoretical probabilities with experimental probabilities.

Note: The answers for Problem 2.1 are based on a container containing 9 red chips, 6 yellow chips, and 3 blue chips. You may, of course, change the contents of the container, but this will change the answers.

Launch (Getting Started)

Connecting to Prior Knowledge

Introduce the idea of theoretical probability before doing the problem. The ideas introduced in the opening paragraphs of Investigation 2 are essential to the unit.

Presenting the Challenge

Have one container that has 9 red chips, 6 yellow chips, and 3 blue chips in it for each group.

Implementation Note: Having one or two containers with a scaled up version of the chips makes for an interesting conversation in the summary: 18 red chips, 12 yellow chips, and 6 blue chips.

Tell the story about the Gee Whiz Everyone Wins! game.

Suggested questions

  • What do you think random means? (a process of selecting or choosing so that each outcome has the same chance of being chosen)
  • Suppose you are a member of the audience. Would you rather be called to the stage first or last? Explain. (Last is better because you can see what results all the other players get before you have to make a choice.)
  • Does it matter that the chip is returned to the container and the contents are mixed up after each contestant? Explain. (Yes, it makes a difference. If the chip is not returned, the number of chips of that color is reduced. So, the probabilities of all the colors change.)
  • Does this appear to be a fair game? (Accept all reasonable conjectures. The question of whether there is an advantage to going first or last is raised again in the Initial Challenge, so you only need to collect first impressions now.)

Some students see no advantage to going last. Throughout this unit, students are asked questions that help them realize that the more information they have, the better their predictions will be. Another way to state this is that the more trials an experimental probability is based on, the closer it will be to the theoretical probability. This concept of the number of trials is developed further through the work in this investigation. (Time)

Have students work in groups of 3–4 students. They will need to assign a leader and complete the experiment 18 times. Explain that after each turn, they must return the container to its original state by replacing the colored chip and then mixing them up. Remind them to keep careful track of the outcome of each choice.
